findings.title: Rate-limit state is only updated in the active-turn loop
findings.description: The new watch channel is now the sole source of truth for presence/alerts, but updates are only applied when `StreamEvent::RateLimit` passes through the active-turn branches. Other stdout consumers in this file, especially `wait_for_permission` and `handle_bg_turn`, still parse and consume events without touching `ratelimit_tx`. That means rate-limit events emitted while the bot is waiting on an interaction approval or while a background turn is running are silently dropped, so Discord presence and threshold alerts can lag or miss real usage entirely.
findings.suggestion: Thread `ratelimit_tx` into every code path that consumes parsed stdout events, or centralize `RateLimit` handling before branching so every parsed event updates the shared state exactly once.

findings.title: Expired reset windows can still fire false threshold alerts
findings.description: `format_presence()` correctly treats a past `five_hour_reset` as 0%, but `check_thresholds()` still compares the raw stored `five_hour_pct` against alert thresholds without checking whether that reset time has already passed. After a restart, or whenever the last seen rate-limit event becomes stale, the monitor can emit a warning for an already-reset bucket even though the status text shows `0%`. The new tests cover stale formatting but do not cover this alert path.
findings.suggestion: Normalize `five_hour_pct` to 0 when `five_hour_reset <= now` before threshold comparison, or ignore threshold evaluation entirely for expired/unknown reset windows.

summary: The diff has two substantive logic regressions in the new watch-based rate-limit path: missed event ingestion outside active turns and false alerts from stale reset data.
